title: Game Theory Through Marine Biology
description: Learn game theory fundamentals and evolutionary applications by exploring strategic interactions in marine ecosystems. This adaptive course routes you to either foundational or advanced content based on your diagnostic assessment.

nodes.title: Game Theory Fundamentals - Pre-Assessment
nodes.content: Test your knowledge of basic game theory concepts in marine environments. This assessment will help guide your learning path.

questions.question: Two fish are deciding whether to cooperate in hunting or compete individually. If both cooperate, they each get 3 units of food. If both compete, they each get 1 unit. If one cooperates and the other competes, the competitor gets 5 units and the cooperator gets 0. This is best described as:
options: A prisoner's dilemma
A dominant strategy for cooperation
An evolutionarily stable strategy
A pure coordination game
feedback: Correct! This is the classic prisoner's dilemma—both have incentive to defect even though mutual cooperation is better.
Not quite. If cooperation were dominant, both would always cooperate.
Not in this case. ESS describes strategies that cannot be invaded, which requires more information.
This isn't about coordination—both players have conflicting incentives.
questions.explanation: The prisoner's dilemma is when mutual cooperation gives better outcomes than mutual defection, but individual incentives push toward defection.

questions.question: A cleaner fish eats parasites off a larger reef fish. The reef fish could try to eat the cleaner fish after it removes parasites. The cleaner fish must trust the reef fish won't do this. What game theory concept best explains why this relationship works?
options: Repeated interaction and reputation effects prevent cheating
Both players have a dominant strategy to cooperate
The cleaner fish is irrational
There is no conflict of interest in this relationship
feedback: Correct! Repeated games and reputational concerns maintain cooperation in symbiotic relationships.
No—in a one-time interaction, both would want to defect.
Game theory assumes rational players.
There IS conflict—the reef fish could benefit from eating the cleaner.
questions.explanation: In repeated games (iterated prisoner's dilemma), reputation and future interactions can sustain cooperation.

questions.question: Two dolphin pods are competing for fish in the same territory. Each can choose to share the area or fight for exclusive access. Fighting is costly but gives exclusive rights. This is an example of:
options: A hawk-dove game
A game with a mixed strategy equilibrium
A cooperative game
A zero-sum game where one player's gain is another's loss
feedback: Correct! The hawk-dove game models conflict over resources when fighting is costly.
True, but hawk-dove is the more specific classification.
Cooperation isn't the natural outcome here—it's about conflict.
While territorial, it's not purely zero-sum since fighting wastes resources.
questions.explanation: The hawk-dove game shows why pure aggression (hawk) and pure avoidance (dove) both fail in equilibrium, leading to mixed strategies.

nodes.title: Game Theory Basics: Competition and Cooperation in the Ocean
nodes.content: Game theory examines how individuals make strategic decisions when outcomes depend on others' choices. In marine ecosystems, organisms face fundamental conflicts between cooperation and self-interest. Dolphins hunting together gain more food than hunting alone, but each dolphin benefits by letting others do the work. Fish competing for reef territory face trade-offs: fighting costs energy but stakes claim to resources. Understanding these strategic interactions reveals why certain behaviors evolve and persist in nature.

nodes.title: Example: Reef Fish Territory Defense
nodes.content: Consider a damselfish defending its algae garden on a coral reef. A rival damselfish appears. The defender faces a choice: fight to maintain territory or flee to search elsewhere. If it fights and wins, it keeps a valuable resource. If it loses, it's injured and loses the territory anyway. The rival faces an inverse choice: attack and risk injury, or retreat to a different reef. Game theory predicts that since fighting is costly but holding territory is valuable, each fish will fight if the territory's value exceeds the expected cost of fighting. Most reef fish solve this by escalating: they display and signal strength before actual combat, reducing costly fights. This is a strategic equilibrium that evolved through repeated interactions.

nodes.title: Evolutionary Game Theory in Marine Ecosystems
nodes.content: Evolutionary game theory extends basic game theory by asking which strategies survive and spread through populations over time. An Evolutionarily Stable Strategy (ESS) is a strategy that, if adopted by most members of a population, cannot be invaded by a rare alternative strategy. In marine ecosystems, this explains why certain behaviors are nearly universal. Sex ratios in fish populations approach 1:1 because populations with other ratios can be invaded by individuals of the rarer sex (who face less competition). Predators and prey engage in endless arms races: if prey evolve camouflage, predators evolve better vision; if predators evolve speed, prey evolve agility. These dynamics create the stunning diversity of marine adaptations. Signaling—like the bright colors of poisonous sea slugs—evolves only when sender and receiver interests align enough to make honest communication stable.

nodes.title: Case Study: Cleaner Fish Symbiosis and the Cheating Problem
nodes.content: Cleaner fish eat parasites from larger reef fish, gaining food while clients gain health. But the system is vulnerable to cheating: a cleaner fish could bite its client's flesh instead of parasites, gaining more food but destroying the relationship. Why does this relationship persist? Game theory reveals the answer: repeated interactions create reputation effects. If a cleaner fish is known to cheat, clients avoid it and it starves. Clients remember which cleaners cheat. Cleaners that maintain honest behavior gain consistent access to clients. This transforms a one-time prisoner's dilemma into a repeated game where cooperation is an ESS. Remarkably, some client fish can recognize individual cleaners and specifically avoid cheaters. This example shows how reputation mechanisms—monitoring, memory, and choice—stabilize cooperation in nature even with conflicting incentives. The system is so effective that cleaner fish stations have become gathering points where predators and prey coexist temporarily.

nodes.title: Advanced Game Theory in Marine Biology—Complete
nodes.content: You've explored how evolutionary game theory explains the complex behaviors and adaptations observed in marine ecosystems. You understand evolutionarily stable strategies, arms races, and how repeated interactions and reputation effects sustain cooperation even with conflicts of interest. These concepts apply across biology: from parasite-host dynamics to mating systems to predator-prey relationships. Game theory reveals that the elaborate behaviors of marine organisms aren't random—they're rational responses to strategic interactions shaped by evolution.

nodes.title: Practice: Ocean Competition Strategies
nodes.content: Apply your understanding of game theory to new marine scenarios.

questions.question: A group of cleaner fish share a reef with many client fish. One cleaner fish notices that clients accept its cheating (biting flesh instead of eating parasites). This cleaner gains more food short-term. Based on game theory, what's the most likely long-term outcome?
options: The cheating cleaner thrives and other cleaners go extinct
The cheating cleaner's reputation spreads and clients avoid all cleaners
Clients remember and avoid the cheating cleaner, and it starves
All cleaner fish eventually cheat and the relationship collapses
feedback: Not in repeated games—reputation matters.
Clients are discriminating—they avoid the specific cheater.
Correct! Individual reputation effects prevent cheating from spreading.
No—if cheaters die, the remaining fish learn to cooperate.
questions.explanation: In repeated interactions, individual reputation is key. Cheaters get punished by being avoided, while honest cooperators thrive.

questions.question: Two sea turtle nesting beaches support competing males during breeding season. Both males could fight to the death for females, or both could use less damaging contests (circling, head-butting). If both fight to death, both die (utility = −10). If both use safe contests, each gets some mating (utility = +5). If one fights while the other contests safely, the fighter wins (utility = +10) but the safe contester still survives and mates (utility = +2). What outcome does game theory predict?
options: All males fight to the death
A mix of fighters and safe contestants emerges in the population
All males use safe contests
Fighting becomes extinct and safe contests dominate
feedback: Fighting to death isn't stable—populations of all-fighters can be invaded by safe contestants.
Correct! This is the hawk-dove game. A mixed strategy is the ESS when pure strategies are exploitable.
A pure dove strategy is unstable—hawks invade and exploit.
While better than all hawks, pure doves are vulnerable to invasion by hawks.
questions.explanation: When both pure strategies are vulnerable to invasion by the other, evolution stabilizes at a mixed strategy where both types persist.

questions.question: Sardine schools have 1,000 fish. If a sardine joins a school, it gains protection from predators (payoff +8) but slightly dilutes everyone's share of food (−1). If it doesn't join, it faces high predation risk (−7) but can forage more (+2). The net payoffs are: join school = +7, don't join = −5. What does game theory predict about sardine behavior?
options: All sardines stay isolated
All sardines join schools
Sardines randomly choose whether to join
Some sardines join and some stay isolated, creating an unstable mix
feedback: Isolation gives negative payoff (−5)—sardines can do better.
Correct! Joining schools is dominant—it's better regardless of others' choices.
There's no randomness here—joining always outperforms isolation.
No—joining strictly dominates staying isolated, so the stable outcome is all-in.
questions.explanation: Schooling is a dominant strategy: joining always gives higher payoff (+7) than isolation (−5), regardless of what others do.

nodes.title: Game Theory Fundamentals—Complete
nodes.content: You've learned the foundations of game theory through marine biology examples. You understand how individuals make strategic choices based on conflicts between self-interest and cooperation, and how these choices shape population-level behavior. From reef fish defending territories to dolphins coordinating hunts to cleaner fish building trust, game theory reveals the logic behind marine animal behavior. You can now recognize strategic interactions in nature and predict how evolution shapes stable behaviors.
